posts.text:
✨ It’s official — I’m in the book 📖✨

When I first sat down for my interview with Izabela Duiwe, I didn’t get the questions in advance — she wanted my story to be as authentic as possible. I came in unprepared, and what followed was an honest, flowing conversation that brought back so many hidden memories I hadn’t realized were still with me.

In my chapter of The Women of Microsoft, I open up about the winding path I’ve taken as both a pianist and a software engineer — two passions that may seem worlds apart, but together taught me discipline, creativity, and resilience. Along the way, I share practical strategies on balancing multiple roles, managing time effectively, and protecting energy to avoid burnout. These reflections were built over years of perseverance, growth, and self-discovery.

This book is more than a publication — it’s a movement to celebrate the voices, journeys, and resilience of women in tech. Being part of this experience, alongside leaders whose journeys deeply inspired me, has been a real privilege. 

Huge thanks to Miri Rodriguez and Izabela Duiwe for bringing these stories to life! 🚀 Launching today! Order your copy here: https://a.co/d/eH9Ky0Y and I am looking forward to hear what you think! #microsoft #microsoft50 #WomenInTech

I get the feeling that this book would fit much better with the Microsoft perception of early 2024. 
Now it’s the Wild West, Squid Games, and environment of 0 transparency and mistrust. 

Still I am sure this is an interesting book, just maybe based on an environment that is no longer there if it ever truly was, sadly.

posts.text:
In "Stay on Fire but Don't Burn Out," passionate Wendy Wang, a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft, intertwines the symphony of code with the melody of piano keys. 
Her journey, from China to U.S., is a dance of fate and fortune, illuminated by acts of kindness. 
Her dream to compete in the Van Cliburn International Piano Competition shimmers like a distant star, revealing the rhythm of balance as the secret to staying aflame without burning out.

Preorder your copy of 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗠𝗶𝗰𝗿𝗼𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁 TODAY! 👉🏼https://a.co/d/bfMTUyO

❤️💚💙💛, 
Izabela & Miri

resharedPost.text:
Today, we celebrate Women's Day, and this year is extra special for me! Our company is marking its 50th anniversary and together with Miri Rodriguez  and 50 Traiblezers we have something unique planned to honor the incredible impact women have had on our company's history and its future.  We'll be sharing more details in the coming months about the idea itself and what you can find in the book, but for now, let's take a moment to celebrate the amazing women at Microsoft!  As March is the third month of the year, I'd like to share my personal three key learnings from this process:

1.	✨Confidence: Embrace your inner strength and believe in your abilities. Confidence is the key to unlocking your potential and empowering yourself to take on new challenges.
2. 📚Competence: Witnessing the exceptional skills and expertise of these women has been truly inspiring. Their dedication and mastery in their fields show that competence knows no bounds. It's about continually learning, growing, and pushing the limits of what you can achieve.
3. ⁠🧗‍♀️🐾 Consistency: Every small step in the right direction brings us closer to our goals. Making those steps with purpose and follow your intuition will help you achieve your dreams. Enjoy the journey and cherish those small victories.


🎉#WomensDay #Microsoft #CelebrateWomen #Inspiration #Empowerment

posts.text:
This #InternationalWomensDay feels extra special—I’m honored to have my story featured in The Women of Microsoft: Empowering Stories from the Minds that Coded the World, authored by the incredible Miri Rodriguez and Izabela Duiwe! As both a software engineer and pianist, I’ve always believed that technology and creativity go hand in hand.

This book shares the journeys of incredible women who have shaped and led Microsoft, proving that passion, resilience, and innovation can break barriers. While I’m grateful to share my own story, what’s even more powerful is seeing the collective wisdom and leadership of so many inspiring women. Their experiences remind us of what’s possible when we lift each other up and push boundaries together.

If you are interested, you are able to pre-order today: https://a.co/d/d8kgJf2

resharedPost.text:
TL;DR: paralyzed vocal cord but also - a hackathon win! 🎉 

It has been a hell of a year. If you don't know, I ended up with a paralyzed vocal cord from covid in April, and while I have my voice pretty much back now (I'll find out next week if no longer paralyzed or just compensating), the middle six months of this year gave me the hardest challenge and greatest opportunity for growth and patience. It was hands down the most difficult, emotional and isolating experience of my life. For fun, if you want to walk a mile in my shoes, commit yourself to only speaking in a whisper next time you're out at a restaurant, concert, conference, class, open house, birthday party, kid's soccer game or any other event where you have to speak at least at a normal volume to be heard. Bet you can't. I won't dare you to go the extra mile and try that for six months. I wouldn't wish it on anyone, not even for a single event. 

I took that pain and uncertainty I was feeling and very timidly and hopefully created a project in Microsoft's annual Hackathon event. One that, if anyone joined the project, would result in something that could support anyone in my position, even temporarily, to ease the burden of working and socializing without a voice. 

Y'all - 20 PEOPLE JOINED THE TEAM!

A core group - Abhishek Reddy Anu Raghavan Javerya Tarique Julie Adam Kipack Jeong Lindsay Greene Maryam Shahid Sylvain Angrignon Tony Julien Wendy Wang - ideated and prototyped (in 3 days!) a phenomenal solution. So phenomenal, in fact, that it won 2nd place in the Hack4Inclusion and 3rd place in the Ability Hack! These wonderful, beautiful people became a team from the jump, and got to work on creating ease and connection in the world. I'm so grateful to this group. 

Our next goal is to try to drive this functionality to become a reality. It will mean the world to me if this labor of love can help even one person. What a hell of a year THAT would be!

#hackathon2023 #Microsoft #inclusion #ability 

Side note: If you or anyone you know is dealing with vocal cord paralysis, here are three things you or they should do today: 
* Join the Vocal Cord Paralysis support group on Facebook - you're not alone.
* Reach out to an opera singer who does singing lessons. VCP often comes with difficulty breathing and shortness of breath, which they can help with. I worked with Leela Subramaniam remotely and she is amazing. Find one who works for you (or I can connect you with Leela!). 
* Reach out to me directly if you need help navigating the healthcare system. There are options in many cases, but the work to find those options can be exhausting. I am happy to provide guidance or help advocate within the system for you to ensure the right questions are asked and answered. I'm also happy to just talk (or text) if you need someone who understands what you're going through.

resharedPost.text:
I rarely reshare content, but this one deeply resonated.

Simon Sinek nails what it means to truly lead. He also nails what it means to truly love.

It's a rare thing that someone can point out something so obviously true but yet so fleeting that you immediately wanna say “This makes so much sense how did I not see this”.

This video also made me very aware of all the little things I am doing, I am not doing and I should be doing. Especially concerning showing appreciation for certain people. 

This video made me reach out to a person from a very vulnerable place and it rekindled a deep connection.

What are some small ways in which you can better show your love / leadership (pick one)?

comments.text: Great vision 👌🏻

Finn Thormeier: Thanks for speaking out of my own heart. Next time someone tries to tell me I care TOO MUCH, I will simply ignore them and move on. Just because others don't care at all doesn't mean it's the right way to move forward. Every company especially those which want to be successful globally shall start at their own doorstep by being an emotionally intelligent employer. If our "company-internal world" is a place everyone loves to be and work, the business world outside is easily conquered. 
This goes nicely hand in hand with Gary Vaynerchuks statement on Culture and Management.

posts.text:
I rarely reshare content, but this one deeply resonated.

Simon Sinek nails what it means to truly lead. He also nails what it means to truly love.

It's a rare thing that someone can point out something so obviously true but yet so fleeting that you immediately wanna say “This makes so much sense how did I not see this”.

This video also made me very aware of all the little things I am doing, I am not doing and I should be doing. Especially concerning showing appreciation for certain people. 

This video made me reach out to a person from a very vulnerable place and it rekindled a deep connection.

What are some small ways in which you can better show your love / leadership (pick one)?
